NKJV.1: My brethren, do not hold the faith of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Lord of glory, with partiality.
NKJV.2: For if there should come into your assembly a man with gold rings, in fine apparel, and there should also come in a poor man in filthy clothes,
NKJV.3: and you pay attention to the one wearing the fine clothes and say to him, “You sit here in a good place,” and say to the poor man, “You stand there,” or, “Sit here at my footstool,”
NKJV.4: have you not shown partiality among yourselves, and become judges with evil thoughts?
NKJV.5: Listen, my beloved brethren: Has God not chosen the poor of this world to be rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom which He promised to those who love Him?
NKJV.6: But you have dishonored the poor man. Do not the rich oppress you and drag you into the courts?
NKJV.7: Do they not blaspheme that noble name by which you are called?
NKJV.8: If you really fulfill the royal law according to the Scripture, “You shall love your neighbor as yourself,” you do well;
NKJV.9: but if you show partiality, you commit sin, and are convicted by the law as transgressors.
NKJV.10: For whoever shall keep the whole law, and yet stumble in one point, he is guilty of all.
NKJV.11: For He who said, “Do not commit adultery,” also said, “Do not murder.” Now if you do not commit adultery, but you do murder, you have become a transgressor of the law.
NKJV.12: So speak and so do as those who will be judged by the law of liberty.
NKJV.13: For judgment is without mercy to the one who has shown no mercy. Mercy triumphs over judgment.
NKJV.14: What does it profit, my brethren, if someone says he has faith but does not have works? Can faith save him?
NKJV.15: If a brother or sister is naked and destitute of daily food,
NKJV.16: and one of you says to them, “Depart in peace, be warmed and filled,” but you do not give them the things which are needed for the body, what does it profit?
NKJV.17: Thus also faith by itself, if it does not have works, is dead.
NKJV.18: But someone will say, “You have faith, and I have works.” Show me your faith without your works, and I will show you my faith by my works.
NKJV.19: You believe that there is one God. You do well. Even the demons believe—and tremble!
NKJV.20: But do you want to know, O foolish man, that faith without works is dead?
NKJV.21: Was not Abraham our father justified by works when he offered Isaac his son on the altar?
NKJV.22: Do you see that faith was working together with his works, and by works faith was made perfect?
NKJV.23: And the Scripture was fulfilled which says, “Abraham believed God, and it was accounted to him for righteousness.” And he was called the friend of God.
NKJV.24: You see then that a man is justified by works, and not by faith only.
NKJV.25: Likewise, was not Rahab the harlot also justified by works when she received the messengers and sent them out another way?
NKJV.26: For as the body without the spirit is dead, so faith without works is dead also.

ESV.1: My brothers,  show no partiality as you hold the faith in our Lord Jesus Christ, the Lord of glory.
ESV.2: For if a man wearing a gold ring and fine clothing comes into your assembly, and a poor man in shabby clothing also comes in,
ESV.3: and if you pay attention to the one who wears the fine clothing and say, “You sit here in a good place,” while you say to the poor man, “You stand over there,” or, “Sit down at my feet,”
ESV.4: have you not then made distinctions among yourselves and become judges with evil thoughts?
ESV.5: Listen, my beloved brothers, has not God chosen those who are poor in the world to be rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom, which he has promised to those who love him?
ESV.6: But you have dishonored the poor man. Are not the rich the ones who oppress you, and the ones who drag you into court?
ESV.7: Are they not the ones who blaspheme the honorable name by which you were called?
ESV.8: If you really fulfill the royal law according to the Scripture, “You shall love your neighbor as yourself,” you are doing well.
ESV.9: But if you show partiality, you are committing sin and are convicted by the law as transgressors.
ESV.10: For whoever keeps the whole law but fails in one point has become guilty of all of it.
ESV.11: For he who said, “Do not commit adultery,” also said, “Do not murder.” If you do not commit adultery but do murder, you have become a transgressor of the law.
ESV.12: So speak and so act as those who are to be judged under the law of liberty.
ESV.13: For judgment is without mercy to one who has shown no mercy. Mercy triumphs over judgment.
ESV.14: What good is it, my brothers, if someone says he has faith but does not have works? Can that faith save him?
ESV.15: If a brother or sister is poorly clothed and lacking in daily food,
ESV.16: and one of you says to them, “Go in peace, be warmed and filled,” without giving them the things needed for the body, what good  is that?
ESV.17: So also faith by itself, if it does not have works, is dead.
ESV.18: But someone will say, “You have faith and I have works.” Show me your faith apart from your works, and I will show you my faith by my works.
ESV.19: You believe that God is one; you do well. Even the demons believe — and shudder!
ESV.20: Do you want to be shown, you foolish person, that faith apart from works is useless?
ESV.21: Was not Abraham our father justified by works when he offered up his son Isaac on the altar?
ESV.22: You see that faith was active along with his works, and faith was completed by his works;
ESV.23: and the Scripture was fulfilled that says, “Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness” — and he was called a friend of God.
ESV.24: You see that a person is justified by works and not by faith alone.
ESV.25: And in the same way was not also Rahab the prostitute justified by works when she received the messengers and sent them out by another way?
ESV.26: For as the body apart from the spirit is dead, so also faith apart from works is dead.

NASB.1: My brethren, do not hold your faith in our glorious Lord Jesus Christ with [an attitude of] personal favoritism.
NASB.2: For if a man comes into your assembly with a gold ring and dressed in fine clothes, and there also comes in a poor man in dirty clothes,
NASB.3: and you pay special attention to the one who is wearing the fine clothes, and say, 'You sit here in a good place,' and you say to the poor man, 'You stand over there, or sit down by my footstool,'
NASB.4: have you not made distinctions among yourselves, and become judges with evil motives?
NASB.5: Listen, my beloved brethren: did not God choose the poor of this world [to be] rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom which He promised to those who love Him?
NASB.6: But you have dishonored the poor man. Is it not the rich who oppress you and personally drag you into court?
NASB.7: Do they not blaspheme the fair name by which you have been called?
NASB.8: If, however, you are fulfilling the royal law according to the Scripture, 'YOU SHALL LOVE YOUR NEIGHBOR AS YOURSELF,' you are doing well.
NASB.9: But if you show partiality, you are committing sin [and] are convicted by the law as transgressors.
NASB.10: For whoever keeps the whole law and yet stumbles in one [point], he has become guilty of all.
NASB.11: For He who said, 'DO NOT COMMIT ADULTERY,' also said, 'DO NOT COMMIT MURDER.' Now if you do not commit adultery, but do commit murder, you have become a transgressor of the law.
NASB.12: So speak and so act as those who are to be judged by [the] law of liberty.
NASB.13: For judgment [will be] merciless to one who has shown no mercy; mercy triumphs over judgment.
NASB.14: What use is it, my brethren, if someone says he has faith but he has no works? Can that faith save him?
NASB.15: If a brother or sister is without clothing and in need of daily food,
NASB.16: and one of you says to them, 'Go in peace, be warmed and be filled,' and yet you do not give them what is necessary for [their] body, what use is that?
NASB.17: Even so faith, if it has no works, is dead, [being] by itself.
NASB.18: But someone may [well] say, 'You have faith and I have works; show me your faith without the works, and I will show you my faith by my works.'
NASB.19: You believe that God is one. You do well; the demons also believe, and shudder.
NASB.20: But are you willing to recognize, you foolish fellow, that faith without works is useless?
NASB.21: Was not Abraham our father justified by works when he offered up Isaac his son on the altar?
NASB.22: You see that faith was working with his works, and as a result of the works, faith was perfected;
NASB.23: and the Scripture was fulfilled which says, 'AND ABRAHAM BELIEVED GOD, AND IT WAS RECKONED TO HIM AS RIGHTEOUSNESS,' and he was called the friend of God.
NASB.24: You see that a man is justified by works and not by faith alone.
NASB.25: In the same way, was not Rahab the harlot also justified by works when she received the messengers and sent them out by another way?
NASB.26: For just as the body without [the] spirit is dead, so also faith without works is dead.

NLT.1: My dear brothers and sisters, how can you claim to have faith in our glorious Lord Jesus Christ if you favor some people over others?
NLT.2: For example, suppose someone comes into your meeting dressed in fancy clothes and expensive jewelry, and another comes in who is poor and dressed in dirty clothes.
NLT.3: If you give special attention and a good seat to the rich person, but you say to the poor one, “You can stand over there, or else sit on the floor” — well,
NLT.4: doesn’t this discrimination show that your judgments are guided by evil motives?
NLT.5: Listen to me, dear brothers and sisters. Hasn’t God chosen the poor in this world to be rich in faith? Aren’t they the ones who will inherit the Kingdom he promised to those who love him?
NLT.6: But you dishonor the poor! Isn’t it the rich who oppress you and drag you into court?
NLT.7: Aren’t they the ones who slander Jesus Christ, whose noble name you bear?
NLT.8: Yes indeed, it is good when you obey the royal law as found in the Scriptures: “Love your neighbor as yourself.”
NLT.9: But if you favor some people over others, you are committing a sin. You are guilty of breaking the law.
NLT.10: For the person who keeps all of the laws except one is as guilty as a person who has broken all of God’s laws.
NLT.11: For the same God who said, “You must not commit adultery,” also said, “You must not murder.” So if you murder someone but do not commit adultery, you have still broken the law.
NLT.12: So whatever you say or whatever you do, remember that you will be judged by the law that sets you free.
NLT.13: There will be no mercy for those who have not shown mercy to others. But if you have been merciful, God will be merciful when he judges you.
NLT.14: What good is it, dear brothers and sisters, if you say you have faith but don’t show it by your actions? Can that kind of faith save anyone?
NLT.15: Suppose you see a brother or sister who has no food or clothing,
NLT.16: and you say, “Good-bye and have a good day; stay warm and eat well” — but then you don’t give that person any food or clothing. What good does that do?
NLT.17: So you see, faith by itself isn’t enough. Unless it produces good deeds, it is dead and useless.
NLT.18: Now someone may argue, “Some people have faith; others have good deeds.” But I say, “How can you show me your faith if you don’t have good deeds? I will show you my faith by my good deeds.”
NLT.19: You say you have faith, for you believe that there is one God. Good for you! Even the demons believe this, and they tremble in terror.
NLT.20: How foolish! Can’t you see that faith without good deeds is useless?
NLT.21: Don’t you remember that our ancestor Abraham was shown to be right with God by his actions when he offered his son Isaac on the altar?
NLT.22: You see, his faith and his actions worked together. His actions made his faith complete.
NLT.23: And so it happened just as the Scriptures say: “Abraham believed God, and God counted him as righteous because of his faith.” He was even called the friend of God.
NLT.24: So you see, we are shown to be right with God by what we do, not by faith alone.
NLT.25: Rahab the prostitute is another example. She was shown to be right with God by her actions when she hid those messengers and sent them safely away by a different road.
NLT.26: Just as the body is dead without breath, so also faith is dead without good works.

WEB.1: My brothers, don’t hold the faith of our Lord Jesus Christ of glory with partiality.
WEB.2: For if a man with a gold ring, in fine clothing, comes into your synagogue,  and a poor man in filthy clothing also comes in;
WEB.3: and you pay special attention to him who wears the fine clothing, and say, “Sit here in a good place”; and you tell the poor man, “Stand there,” or “Sit by my footstool”;
WEB.4: haven’t you shown partiality among yourselves, and become judges with evil thoughts?
WEB.5: Listen, my beloved brothers. Didn’t God choose those who are poor in this world to be rich in faith, and heirs of the Kingdom which he promised to those who love him?
WEB.6: But you have dishonored the poor man. Don’t the rich oppress you, and personally drag you before the courts?
WEB.7: Don’t they blaspheme the honorable name by which you are called?
WEB.8: However, if you fulfill the royal law, according to the Scripture, “You shall love your neighbor as yourself,”  you do well.
WEB.9: But if you show partiality, you commit sin, being convicted by the law as transgressors.
WEB.10: For whoever keeps the whole law, and yet stumbles in one point, he has become guilty of all.
WEB.11: For he who said, “Do not commit adultery,”  also said, “Do not commit murder.”  Now if you do not commit adultery, but murder, you have become a transgressor of the law.
WEB.12: So speak, and so do, as men who are to be judged by a law of freedom.
WEB.13: For judgment is without mercy to him who has shown no mercy. Mercy triumphs over judgment.
WEB.14: What good is it, my brothers, if a man says he has faith, but has no works? Can faith save him?
WEB.15: And if a brother or sister is naked and in lack of daily food,
WEB.16: and one of you tells them, “Go in peace, be warmed and filled”; and yet you didn’t give them the things the body needs, what good is it?
WEB.17: Even so faith, if it has no works, is dead in itself.
WEB.18: Yes, a man will say, “You have faith, and I have works.” Show me your faith without works, and I by my works will show you my faith.
WEB.19: You believe that God is one. You do well. The demons also believe, and shudder.
WEB.20: But do you want to know, vain man, that faith apart from works is dead?
WEB.21: Wasn’t Abraham our father justified by works, in that he offered up Isaac his son on the altar?
WEB.22: You see that faith worked with his works, and by works faith was perfected;
WEB.23: and the Scripture was fulfilled which says, “Abraham believed God, and it was accounted to him as righteousness”;  and he was called the friend of God.
WEB.24: You see then that by works, a man is justified, and not only by faith.
WEB.25: In the same way, wasn’t Rahab the prostitute also justified by works, in that she received the messengers, and sent them out another way?
WEB.26: For as the body apart from the spirit is dead, even so faith apart from works is dead.

CSB17.1: My brothers and sisters, do not show favoritism as you hold on to the faith in our glorious Lord Jesus Christ. <sup>ⓖ</sup>
CSB17.2: For if someone comes into your meeting wearing a gold ring and dressed in fine clothes, and a poor person dressed in filthy clothes also comes in,
CSB17.3: if you look with favor on the one wearing the fine clothes and say, “Sit here in a good place,” and yet you say to the poor person, “Stand over there,” or “Sit here on the floor by my footstool,”
CSB17.4: haven’t you made distinctions among yourselves and become judges with evil thoughts?
CSB17.5: Listen, my dear brothers and sisters: Didn’t God choose the poor in this world <sup>ⓗ</sup> to be rich in faith <sup>ⓘ</sup> and heirs <sup>ⓙ</sup> of the kingdom that he has promised to those who love him?
CSB17.6: Yet you have dishonored the poor. <sup>ⓚ</sup> Don’t the rich oppress you and drag <sup>ⓛ</sup> you into court?
CSB17.7: Don’t they blaspheme the good name that was invoked over you? <sup>ⓜ</sup>
CSB17.8: Indeed, if you fulfill the royal law prescribed in the Scripture, Love your neighbor as yourself, [1] ⓝ you are doing well.
CSB17.9: If, however, you show favoritism, <sup>ⓐ</sup> you commit sin and are convicted by the law as transgressors.
CSB17.10: For whoever keeps the entire law, and yet stumbles at one point, is guilty of breaking it all. <sup>ⓑ</sup>
CSB17.11: For he who said, Do not commit adultery, [2] also said, Do not murder. [3] ⓒ So if you do not commit adultery, but you murder, you are a lawbreaker.
CSB17.12: Speak and act as those who are to be judged by the law of freedom. <sup>ⓓ</sup>
CSB17.13: For judgment is without mercy to the one who has not shown mercy. <sup>ⓔ</sup> Mercy triumphs over judgment.
CSB17.14: What good is it, my brothers and sisters, if someone claims to have faith but does not have works? Can such faith save him?
CSB17.15: If a brother or sister is without clothes and lacks daily food
CSB17.16: and one of you says to them, “Go in peace, stay warm, and be well fed,” but you don’t give them what the body needs, what good is it? <sup>ⓕ</sup>
CSB17.17: In the same way faith, if it doesn’t have works, is dead by itself.
CSB17.18: But someone will say, “You have faith, and I have works.” <sup>[4]</sup> Show me your faith without works, and I will show you faith by my works. <sup>ⓖ</sup>
CSB17.19: You believe that God is one. Good! Even the demons believe — and they shudder. <sup>ⓗ</sup>
CSB17.20: Senseless person! Are you willing to learn that faith without works is useless?
CSB17.21: Wasn’t Abraham our father justified by works in offering Isaac his son on the altar?
CSB17.22: You see that faith was active together with his works, and by works, faith was made complete, <sup>ⓘ</sup>
CSB17.23: and the Scripture was fulfilled that says, Abraham believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness, [5] ⓙ and he was called God’s friend. ⓚ
CSB17.24: You see that a person is justified by works and not by faith alone.
CSB17.25: In the same way, wasn’t Rahab the prostitute also justified by works in receiving the messengers and sending them out by a different route? <sup>ⓛ</sup>
CSB17.26: For just as the body without the spirit is dead, so also faith without works is dead.

AMP.1: My fellow believers, do not practice your faith in our glorious Lord Jesus Christ with an attitude of partiality [toward people—show no favoritism, no prejudice, no snobbery].
AMP.2: For if a man comes into your meeting place wearing a gold ring and fine clothes, and a poor man in dirty clothes also comes in,
AMP.3: and you pay special attention to the one who wears the fine clothes, and say to him, "You sit here in this good seat," and you tell the poor man, "You stand over there, or sit down [on the floor] by my footstool,"
AMP.4: have you not discriminated among yourselves, and become judges with wrong motives?
AMP.5: Listen, my beloved brothers and sisters: has not God chosen the poor of this world to be rich in faith and [as believers to be] heirs of the kingdom which He promised to those who love Him?
AMP.6: But you [in contrast] have dishonored the poor man. Is it not the rich who oppress and exploit you, and personally drag you into the courts of law?
AMP.7: Do they not blaspheme the precious name [of Christ] by which you are called?
AMP.8: If, however, you are [really] fulfilling the royal law according to the Scripture, "You shall love your neighbor as yourself [that is, if you have an unselfish concern for others and do things for their benefit]" you are doing well.
AMP.9: But if you show partiality [prejudice, favoritism], you are committing sin and are convicted by the Law as offenders.
AMP.10: For whoever keeps the whole Law but stumbles in one point, he has become guilty of [breaking] all of it.
AMP.11: For He who said, "Do not commit adultery," also said, "Do not murder." Now if you do not commit adultery, but you murder, you have become guilty of transgressing the [entire] Law.
AMP.12: Speak and act [consistently] as people who are going to be judged by the law of liberty [that moral law that frees obedient Christians from the bondage of sin].
AMP.13: For judgment will be merciless to one who has shown no mercy; but [to the one who has shown mercy] mercy triumphs [victoriously] over judgment.
AMP.14: What is the benefit, my fellow believers, if someone claims to have faith but has no [good] works [as evidence]? Can that [kind of] faith save him? [No, a mere claim of faith is not sufficient—genuine faith produces good works.]
AMP.15: If a brother or sister is without [adequate] clothing and lacks [enough] food for each day,
AMP.16: and one of you says to them, "Go in peace [with my blessing], [keep] warm and feed yourselves," but he does not give them the necessities for the body, what good does that do?
AMP.17: So too, faith, if it does not have works [to back it up], is by itself dead [inoperative and ineffective].
AMP.18: But someone may say, "You [claim to] have faith and I have [good] works; show me your [alleged] faith without the works [if you can], and I will show you my faith by my works [that is, by what I do]."
AMP.19: You believe that God is one; you do well [to believe that]. The demons also believe [that], and shudder and bristle [in awe-filled terror—they have seen His wrath]!
AMP.20: But are you willing to recognize, you foolish [spiritually shallow] person, that faith without [good] works is useless?
AMP.21: Was our father Abraham not [shown to be] justified by works [of obedience which expressed his faith] when he offered Isaac his son on the altar [as a sacrifice to God]?
AMP.22: You see that [his] faith was working together with his works, and as a result of the works, his faith was completed [reaching its maturity when he expressed his faith through obedience].
AMP.23: And the Scripture was fulfilled which says, "Abraham believed God, and this [faith] was credited to him [by God] as righteousness and as conformity to His will," and he was called the friend of God.
AMP.24: You see that a man (believer) is justified by works and not by faith alone [that is, by acts of obedience a born-again believer reveals his faith].
AMP.25: In the same way, was Rahab the prostitute not justified by works too, when she received the [Hebrew] spies as guests and protected them, and sent them away [to escape] by a different route?
AMP.26: For just as the [human] body without the spirit is dead, so faith without works [of obedience] is also dead.

MSG.1: My dear friends, don't let public opinion influence how you live out our glorious, Christ-originated faith.
MSG.2: If a man enters your church wearing an expensive suit, and a street person wearing rags comes in right after him,
MSG.3: and you say to the man in the suit, "Sit here, sir; this is the best seat in the house!" and either ignore the street person or say, "Better sit here in the back row,"
MSG.4: haven't you segregated God's children and proved that you are judges who can't be trusted?
MSG.5: Listen, dear friends. Isn't it clear by now that God operates quite differently? He chose the world's down-and-out as the kingdom's first citizens, with full rights and privileges. This kingdom is promised to anyone who loves God.
MSG.6: And here you are abusing these same citizens! Isn't it the high and mighty who exploit you, who use the courts to rob you blind?
MSG.7: Aren't they the ones who scorn the new name—"Christian"—used in your baptisms?
MSG.8: You do well when you complete the Royal Rule of the Scriptures: "Love others as you love yourself."
MSG.9: But if you play up to these so-called important people, you go against the Rule and stand convicted by it.
MSG.10: You can't pick and choose in these things, specializing in keeping one or two things in God's law and ignoring others.
MSG.11: The same God who said, "Don't commit adultery," also said, "Don't murder." If you don't commit adultery but go ahead and murder, do you think your non-adultery will cancel out your murder? No, you're a murderer, period.
MSG.12: Talk and act like a person expecting to be judged by the Rule that sets us free.
MSG.13: For if you refuse to act kindly, you can hardly expect to be treated kindly. Kind mercy wins over harsh judgment every time.
MSG.14: Dear friends, do you think you'll get anywhere in this if you learn all the right words but never do anything? Does merely talking about faith indicate that a person really has it?
MSG.15: For instance, you come upon an old friend dressed in rags and half-starved
MSG.16: and say, "Good morning, friend! Be clothed in Christ! Be filled with the Holy Spirit!" and walk off without providing so much as a coat or a cup of soup—where does that get you?
MSG.17: Isn't it obvious that God-talk without God-acts is outrageous nonsense?
MSG.18: I can already hear one of you agreeing by saying, "Sounds good. You take care of the faith department, I'll handle the works department." Not so fast. You can no more show me your works apart from your faith than I can show you my faith apart from my works. Faith and works, works and faith, fit together hand in glove.
MSG.19: Do I hear you professing to believe in the one and only God, but then observe you complacently sitting back as if you had done something wonderful? That's just great. Demons do that, but what good does it do them?
MSG.20: Use your heads! Do you suppose for a minute that you can cut faith and works in two and not end up with a corpse on your hands?
MSG.21: Wasn't our ancestor Abraham "made right with God by works" when he placed his son Isaac on the sacrificial altar?
MSG.22: Isn't it obvious that faith and works are yoked partners, that faith expresses itself in works? That the works are "works of faith"?
MSG.23: The full meaning of "believe" in the Scripture sentence, "Abraham believed God and was set right with God," includes his action. It's that mesh of believing and acting that got Abraham named "God's friend."
MSG.24: Is it not evident that a person is made right with God not by a barren faith but by faith fruitful in works?
MSG.25: The same with Rahab, the Jericho harlot. Wasn't her action in hiding God's spies and helping them escape—that seamless unity of believing and doing—what counted with God?
MSG.26: The very moment you separate body and spirit, you end up with a corpse. Separate faith and works and you get the same thing: a corpse.

NIV.1:
Favoritism Forbidden
My brothers, as believers in our glorious Lord Jesus Christ, don’t show favoritism.
NIV.2: Suppose a man comes into your meeting wearing a gold ring and fine clothes, and a poor man in shabby clothes also comes in.
NIV.3: If you show special attention to the man wearing fine clothes and say, “Here’s a good seat for you,” but say to the poor man, “You stand there” or “Sit on the floor by my feet,”
NIV.4: have you not discriminated among yourselves and become judges with evil thoughts?
NIV.5: Listen, my dear brothers: Has not God chosen those who are poor in the eyes of the world to be rich in faith and to inherit the kingdom he promised those who love him?
NIV.6: But you have insulted the poor. Is it not the rich who are exploiting you? Are they not the ones who are dragging you into court?
NIV.7: Are they not the ones who are slandering the noble name of him to whom you belong?
NIV.8: If you really keep the royal law found in Scripture, “Love your neighbor as yourself,” you are doing right.
NIV.9: But if you show favoritism, you sin and are convicted by the law as lawbreakers.
NIV.10: For whoever keeps the whole law and yet stumbles at just one point is guilty of breaking all of it.
NIV.11: For he who said, “Do not commit adultery,” also said, “Do not murder.” If you do not commit adultery but do commit murder, you have become a lawbreaker.
NIV.12: Speak and act as those who are going to be judged by the law that gives freedom,
NIV.13: because judgment without mercy will be shown to anyone who has not been merciful. Mercy triumphs over judgment!
NIV.14:
Faith and Deeds
What good is it, my brothers, if a man claims to have faith but has no deeds? Can such faith save him?
NIV.15: Suppose a brother or sister is without clothes and daily food.
NIV.16: If one of you says to him, “Go, I wish you well; keep warm and well fed,” but does nothing about his physical needs, what good is it?
NIV.17: In the same way, faith by itself, if it is not accompanied by action, is dead.
NIV.18:
But someone will say, “You have faith; I have deeds.”
Show me your faith without deeds, and I will show you my faith by what I do.
NIV.19: You believe that there is one God. Good! Even the demons believe that—and shudder.
NIV.20: You foolish man, do you want evidence that faith without deeds is useless?
NIV.21: Was not our ancestor Abraham considered righteous for what he did when he offered his son Isaac on the altar?
NIV.22: You see that his faith and his actions were working together, and his faith was made complete by what he did.
NIV.23: And the scripture was fulfilled that says, “Abraham believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness,” and he was called God’s friend.
NIV.24: You see that a person is justified by what he does and not by faith alone.
NIV.25: In the same way, was not even Rahab the prostitute considered righteous for what she did when she gave lodging to the spies and sent them off in a different direction?
NIV.26: As the body without the spirit is dead, so faith without deeds is dead.

NRSVCE.1: My brothers and sisters, do you with your acts of favoritism really believe in our glorious Lord Jesus Christ?
NRSVCE.2: For if a person with gold rings and in fine clothes comes into your assembly, and if a poor person in dirty clothes also comes in,
NRSVCE.3: and if you take notice of the one wearing the fine clothes and say, “Have a seat here, please,” while to the one who is poor you say, “Stand there,” or, “Sit at my feet,”
NRSVCE.4: have you not made distinctions among yourselves, and become judges with evil thoughts?
NRSVCE.5: Listen, my beloved brothers and sisters. Has not God chosen the poor in the world to be rich in faith and to be heirs of the kingdom that he has promised to those who love him?
NRSVCE.6: But you have dishonored the poor. Is it not the rich who oppress you? Is it not they who drag you into court?
NRSVCE.7: Is it not they who blaspheme the excellent name that was invoked over you?
NRSVCE.8: You do well if you really fulfill the royal law according to the scripture, “You shall love your neighbor as yourself.”
NRSVCE.9: But if you show partiality, you commit sin and are convicted by the law as transgressors.
NRSVCE.10: For whoever keeps the whole law but fails in one point has become accountable for all of it.
NRSVCE.11: For the one who said, “You shall not commit adultery,” also said, “You shall not murder.” Now if you do not commit adultery but if you murder, you have become a transgressor of the law.
NRSVCE.12: So speak and so act as those who are to be judged by the law of liberty.
NRSVCE.13: For judgment will be without mercy to anyone who has shown no mercy; mercy triumphs over judgment.
NRSVCE.14: What good is it, my brothers and sisters, if you say you have faith but do not have works? Can faith save you?
NRSVCE.15: If a brother or sister is naked and lacks daily food,
NRSVCE.16: and one of you says to them, “Go in peace; keep warm and eat your fill,” and yet you do not supply their bodily needs, what is the good of that?
NRSVCE.17: So faith by itself, if it has no works, is dead.
NRSVCE.18: But someone will say, “You have faith and I have works.” Show me your faith apart from your works, and I by my works will show you my faith.
NRSVCE.19: You believe that God is one; you do well. Even the demons believe — and shudder.
NRSVCE.20: Do you want to be shown, you senseless person, that faith apart from works is barren?
NRSVCE.21: Was not our ancestor Abraham justified by works when he offered his son Isaac on the altar?
NRSVCE.22: You see that faith was active along with his works, and faith was brought to completion by the works.
NRSVCE.23: Thus the scripture was fulfilled that says, “Abraham believed God, and it was reckoned to him as righteousness,” and he was called the friend of God.
NRSVCE.24: You see that a person is justified by works and not by faith alone.
NRSVCE.25: Likewise, was not Rahab the prostitute also justified by works when she welcomed the messengers and sent them out by another road?
NRSVCE.26: For just as the body without the spirit is dead, so faith without works is also dead.

NET.1: My brothers and sisters, do not show prejudice if you possess faith in our glorious Lord Jesus Christ.
NET.2: For if someone comes into your assembly wearing a gold ring and fine clothing, and a poor person enters in filthy clothes,
NET.3: do you pay attention to the one who is finely dressed and say, “You sit here in a good place,” and to the poor person, “You stand over there,” or “Sit on the floor”?
NET.4: If so, have you not made distinctions among yourselves and become judges with evil motives?
NET.5: Listen, my dear brothers and sisters! Did not God choose the poor in the world to be rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom that he promised to those who love him?
NET.6: But you have dishonored the poor! Are not the rich oppressing you and dragging you into the courts?
NET.7: Do they not blaspheme the good name of the one you belong to?
NET.8: But if you fulfill the royal law as expressed in this scripture, “You shall love your neighbor as yourself,” you are doing well.
NET.9: But if you show prejudice, you are committing sin and are convicted by the law as violators.
NET.10: For the one who obeys the whole law but fails in one point has become guilty of all of it.
NET.11: For he who said, “Do not commit adultery,” also said, “Do not murder.” Now if you do not commit adultery but do commit murder, you have become a violator of the law.
NET.12: Speak and act as those who will be judged by a law that gives freedom.
NET.13: For judgment is merciless for the one who has shown no mercy. But mercy triumphs over judgment.
NET.14: What good is it, my brothers and sisters, if someone claims to have faith but does not have works? Can this kind of faith save him?
NET.15: If a brother or sister is poorly clothed and lacks daily food,
NET.16: and one of you says to them, “Go in peace, keep warm and eat well,” but you do not give them what the body needs, what good is it?
NET.17: So also faith, if it does not have works, is dead being by itself.
NET.18: But someone will say, “You have faith and I have works.” Show me your faith without works and I will show you faith by my works.
NET.19: You believe that God is one; well and good. Even the demons believe that — and tremble with fear.
NET.20: But would you like evidence, you empty fellow, that faith without works is useless?
NET.21: Was not Abraham our father justified by works when he offered Isaac his son on the altar?
NET.22: You see that his faith was working together with his works and his faith was perfected by works.
NET.23: And the scripture was fulfilled that says, “Now Abraham believed God and it was counted to him for righteousness,” and he was called God’s friend.
NET.24: You see that a person is justified by works and not by faith alone.
NET.25: And similarly, was not Rahab the prostitute also justified by works when she welcomed the messengers and sent them out by another way?
NET.26: For just as the body without the spirit is dead, so also faith without works is dead.
